content:
New Blood or Old Guard? Supplier Churn at Burnley

Key Findings

  • Burnley Borough Council spent a total of £355.2M across 30,580 transactions over five financial years.
  • The council engaged with 4,485 unique suppliers between 2021/22 and 2025/26.
  • Overall spending increased by £6.2M, representing a 10.1% rise from 2021 to 2025.
  • The largest annual spending increase was +£27.6M, a +50.3% jump from 2022 to 2023.
  • The most significant annual spending decrease was £-19,767,368, a -22.6% reduction from 2024 to 2025.

Burnley's Spending Landscape and Supplier Engagement

Burnley Borough Council managed a substantial financial portfolio over the five financial years from 2021/22 to 2025/26. The council's total expenditure reached £355.2M, distributed across 30,580 individual transactions. This significant sum highlights the scale of public services and projects delivered within the borough during this period. The data shows a broad engagement with the market, involving a considerable number of different organisations and businesses.

Over these five years, Burnley Borough Council worked with 4,485 unique suppliers. This large number of distinct vendors suggests a varied approach to procurement, potentially encompassing a mix of long-term strategic partners and numerous one-off or short-term engagements for specific needs. The volume of unique suppliers relative to the total number of transactions indicates that many suppliers may have been involved in only a few transactions, contributing to a dynamic supplier landscape.

Fluctuations in Annual Expenditure

An analysis of Burnley Borough Council's annual spending reveals significant fluctuations over the five-year period. The overall trend shows an increase of £6.2M from 2021 to 2025, representing a 10.1% rise. However, this overall growth masks considerable year-on-year volatility, with periods of both substantial increases and notable decreases in expenditure. Understanding these shifts can offer insights into the council's operational priorities and project cycles.

The council's spending patterns demonstrate a varied financial trajectory. Expenditure initially decreased from £61.4M in 2021 to £54.9M in 2022, a reduction of £-6,551,481 or -10.7%. This was followed by a sharp increase, with spending rising by +£27.6M, or +50.3%, from 2022 to 2023, reaching £82.5M. A further increase of +£4.9M, or +6.0%, was observed from 2023 to 2024, pushing the annual spend to £87.4M. These periods of growth suggest significant project initiation or increased service delivery demands.

The most recent period analysed shows a substantial contraction in spending. From 2024 to 2025, expenditure decreased by £-19,767,368, a -22.6% drop, bringing the annual total to £67.6M. Such a significant reduction could indicate the completion of major projects, a reprioritisation of funds, or a shift in procurement strategies. These year-on-year changes highlight the dynamic nature of public spending and its potential impact on the council's engagement with its extensive network of suppliers.

Financial Year Total Spend Year-on-Year Change Percentage Change
2021/22 £61.4M N/A N/A
2022/23 £54.9M £-6,551,481 -10.7%
2023/24 £82.5M +£27.6M +50.3%
2024/25 £87.4M +£4.9M +6.0%
2025/26 £67.6M £-19,767,368 -22.6%

Understanding Supplier Dynamics

The high number of unique suppliers, 4,485, across 30,580 transactions over five years, suggests a complex and potentially fluid supplier ecosystem for Burnley Borough Council. While the data does not directly detail supplier retention or the proportion of new versus long-term vendors, the sheer volume implies that many suppliers may be engaged for specific, often short-term, projects or services. This pattern could contribute to what might be perceived as supplier churn, where new suppliers are regularly brought in for new requirements, and older ones conclude their engagements.

The significant year-on-year spending fluctuations could also influence supplier dynamics. For instance, the substantial increase of +£27.6M from 2022 to 2023 might have necessitated engaging new suppliers for expanded projects or services. Conversely, the sharp decrease of £-19,767,368 from 2024 to 2025 could reflect the completion of major contracts, potentially leading to reduced engagement with certain suppliers or a shift in the supplier base. Analysing the specific contracts associated with these spending changes would provide a clearer picture of whether these shifts represent new blood or a change in the old guard.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Burnley Borough Council about the reasons behind the significant year-on-year spending fluctuations and how new suppliers are selected.
  • Attend council committee meetings (they are open to the public) to hear discussions about procurement and financial planning.
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see details of awarded contracts and their durations.
  • Write to your local councillor about the council's supplier engagement strategy and how it ensures value for money amidst spending changes.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data for Burnley Borough Council and other Lancashire councils yourself.
This article is based on Burnley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
